hub_subtitle: Business English para as reuniões com o time Global da Sanofi, congressos médicos e a apresentação de agosto

lesson.menu_title: The Rain Plan -- Presenting Upcoming Launches and the Backup Plan to Global
lesson.menu_desc: A Global não quer só o plano; quer o plano B. A aula do PRIMEIRO CONDICIONAL -- 'If the launch is delayed, we WILL use the backup plan' -- para apresentar os próximos lançamentos e, ao lado, o Rain Plan: o que você fará SE algo mudar. A linguagem de quem planeja com honestidade -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 18 -- The Rain Plan
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Emmanuele Orrico | Lesson 18 -- The Rain Plan
lesson.grammar_point: first conditional (if + present simple, will + base form)

listenings.text: Hi Emmanuele, it's Marcus from the Global team. Quick one before Thursday. On Thursday we want to hear your Rain Plan for the Q3 launch. Two things, keep it simple. First, the normal plan: what happens if everything goes well, the standard rollout. And second, the backup plan: if the launch is delayed, what will you do? We know things change, so we want to see that you are ready. If you have a good backup plan, the Global team will feel confident. Send the slide in advance if you can. Thanks, and talk on Thursday.

Okay, let me close the launch part. Here is where we landed. The normal plan is clear: we are going to launch in Q3, on schedule. But we agreed on a strong backup plan. If the product approval is late, we will postpone the launch by one month, and we will reschedule the congress for November. If we are behind schedule in October, we will start with the private market first. And if it rains on launch day, we will move the event online. Emmanuele will send the updated Rain Plan slide by Friday, in advance of the board meeting. Good work, everyone. A plan with a backup plan is a plan we can trust.

extra_audio.text: The normal plan is to launch in Q3, on schedule. But if the product approval is late, we will postpone the launch by one month. If we are behind schedule, we will start with the private market first. If it rains on launch day, we will move the event online. And if the numbers are strong, we will expand the rollout, ahead of schedule.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a second plan you use if the first plan does not work
a problem that stops progress for a while
extra time before something happens; when something is late
to move something to a later date
to give something a new date or time
before the time something happens; early, with time to spare

reading.rtitle: The Plan, and the Plan Behind the Plan
paras: Every quarter, Emmanuele presents the upcoming launches to the Global team. She shows the calendar: the new product is going to launch in Q3, on schedule. That is the normal plan, and it is clear. But the Global team always asks one more question, the hard one: "And if something changes? What is your backup plan?" A good manager never answers "I don't know." She has a Rain Plan.

A Rain Plan is the plan behind the plan. It answers a simple question: if X happens, what will we do? "If the product approval is late, we will postpone the launch by one month." "If we are behind schedule in October, we will start with the private market first." "If it rains on launch day, we will move the event online." Notice the shape of every sentence: it has two parts. The first part starts with if and uses the present -- if the launch is late, if the numbers are strong. The second part uses will -- we will postpone, we will move, we will expand. If this, then we will do that.

This is not being negative. It is the opposite: a manager who has a backup plan for every setback is a manager the Global team trusts. A delay is not a disaster if you already know your next move. "If everything goes well, we will expand ahead of schedule. And if there is a problem, we will not stop -- we have a Rain Plan." The normal plan shows what you hope. The Rain Plan shows that you are ready. And being ready, in English or in any language, is what leadership sounds like.

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: A good manager should only present the normal plan and never think about problems.
A good manager presents the plan AND a backup plan (a Rain Plan) -- "if X happens, we will do Y" -- and that readiness builds trust.
A Rain Plan means the launch will definitely fail, so it is better not to launch.

items: The text says the Global team only wants to hear the normal plan.
f
The opposite: they always ask "And if something changes? What is your backup plan?"
A Rain Plan answers the question: if X happens, what will we do?
t
"It answers a simple question: if X happens, what will we do?"
In the sentences, the part after "if" uses "will".
f
The text says the part after if uses the PRESENT (if the launch is late), and the other part uses will.
According to the text, having a backup plan builds trust with the Global team.
t
"A manager who has a backup plan for every setback is a manager the Global team trusts."

parts: Emmanuele closes her Rain Plan: "The normal plan is to launch in Q3. But if the approval
postpone the launch by one month. If we are
schedule, we will start with the private market first. And if the numbers
strong, we will expand the rollout,

gapfill.text: The first conditional has two parts. Part 1: if + the present simple -- if the approval IS late, if we ARE behind schedule, if the numbers ARE strong (present, never "will"). Part 2: will + the base verb -- we WILL postpone, we WILL start, we WILL expand. If this (present), then we will do that (will + base). The order can switch: "We will postpone it if the approval is late." Keep going to for the plan you already decided ("We are going to launch in Q3"); use if + will for the backup plan ("If the launch is delayed, we will postpone it").

bank.label: Useful language for a plan and its backup plan
items: The normal plan is to launch in Q3, on schedule.
But we also have a backup plan.
If the launch is delayed, we will use the backup plan.
If we are behind schedule, we will postpone the congress.
If it rains on launch day, we will move the event online.
If the numbers are strong, we will expand ahead of schedule.
If everything goes well, we won't need the Rain Plan.

lf.title: Which is it -- a decided plan, or a condition with its result?
items: A
We are going to launch in Q3.
-- a decided plan (going to)
soft
B
If the launch is delayed, we will postpone it.
-- a condition + result (first conditional)
strong
C
If it rains, we will move the event online.
-- a condition + result (first conditional)
strong
D
We are going to present the calendar on Thursday.
-- a decided plan (going to)
soft

lf.text: A and D use GOING TO -- a plan already decided, fixed on the calendar. B and C use the FIRST CONDITIONAL -- if + present (if the launch is delayed, if it rains), then will + base verb (we will postpone, we will move). A real Rain Plan mixes both: the decided plan with going to, and the backup plan with if + will.

items.situation: The board asks: "What will you do if the launch is delayed?" Give your backup plan.
tips: If the launch is delayed, we will postpone it by one month.
If the approval is late, we will use the backup plan.

items.situation: The launch event is outside, and rain is possible. Give the backup.
tips: If it rains on launch day, we will move the event online.
If the weather is bad, we won't cancel -- we will go online.

items.situation: You are behind schedule in October. Tell the team your plan.
tips: If we are behind schedule, we will start with the private market first.
If we are late, we will reschedule the congress for November.

items.situation: The early numbers look strong. Say what you will do.
tips: If the numbers are strong, we will expand the rollout to more regions.
If demand is high, we will launch ahead of schedule.
